title: The Timed Up and Go (TUG) Test: How to Perform and Interpret It
description: Learn how to perform the Timed Up and Go test, interpret results, and understand what TUG scores tell you about fall risk and functional mobility.

blocks.question: What is the Timed Up and Go (TUG) test?
blocks.directAnswer: The Timed Up and Go test measures functional mobility by timing how long it takes to stand from a chair, walk 3 meters, turn around, walk back, and sit. Results above 14 seconds indicate elevated fall risk. The TUG test has 97% sensitivity for identifying mobility problems and is Grade 1B evidence in fall risk assessment.

tldrBullets.text: TUG measures real-world functional mobility in under one minute
Normal result is typically under 12 seconds; elevated risk is above 14 seconds
TUG is validated for detecting fall risk with 97% sensitivity
Most useful when combined with grip strength and gait speed assessment

blocks.expandedAnswer:
The Timed Up and Go test is a performance-based functional assessment designed to measure mobility in older adults and those with functional limitations. It was originally described by Podsiadlo and Richardson in 1991 as a quick, practical test suitable for use in clinical settings.

The test is simple but informative. A patient stands from a standard chair, walks a short distance at their normal pace, turns around, and returns to sit. The time taken reflects multiple aspects of functional ability: leg strength, balance, coordination, and walking speed. Slower times indicate greater fall risk and mobility impairment.

The TUG has become a standard assessment in geriatrics, fall risk programs, and rehabilitation. It requires minimal equipment and can be performed in any clinic space, making it suitable for widespread clinical use.

sources.title: The Timed Up and Go: a test of basic functional mobility for frail elderly persons
World Guidelines for the Prevention of Falls in Older Adults and with Osteoporosis
The European Union Geriatric Medicine Society position on assessment tools for fall risk

blocks.body:
What the TUG test measures

The Timed Up and Go test assesses functional mobility. This is distinct from static strength or balance. Functional mobility means the ability to move safely and efficiently in real-world situations.

The TUG test combines multiple components of function. Standing from a chair without using arms requires leg strength and balance. Walking requires adequate lower limb strength, proprioception, and cardiovascular function. Turning requires dynamic balance and coordination. The entire task requires cognitive processing and motor planning.

A slow TUG time indicates impairment in one or more of these components. The test does not identify which specific component is impaired, but slower times indicate greater overall mobility limitation and higher fall risk.

The TUG test is validated for detecting fall risk. People who take more than 14 seconds have substantially elevated risk of falling. This cutoff has been validated in multiple populations across different settings.

How to perform the Timed Up and Go test

The TUG test requires minimal equipment. A standard armchair (approximately 46 cm seat height), a clear walking path, and a means of timing (stopwatch or smartphone timer) are all that is needed.

Setup: Position the chair against a wall. Mark a walking path 3 meters from the front of the chair and ensure the path is clear of obstacles. Ensure the patient is wearing their usual footwear and glasses or hearing aids if normally used.

Instructions to the patient: Tell them clearly what you want them to do: 'When I say go, I want you to stand up from the chair, walk at your normal pace to the line on the floor 3 meters away, turn around and come back, and sit down in the chair. I will be timing how long it takes. Ready, go.'

Timing starts when the patient's bottom leaves the chair. The patient walks to the 3-meter mark, turns around, and walks back to the chair. Timing stops when the patient's bottom touches the chair on return.

Safety considerations: Stand close to the patient initially to ensure safety, particularly if they appear unsteady. Have a hand available to stabilize them if needed. If the patient falls or appears to be falling, support them immediately and stop the test.

Perform the test once under observation. Some protocols include repeating the test twice and using the faster result, though a single performance is acceptable for clinical use. Document any observations during the test, such as unsteadiness, hesitation, use of an assistive device, or loss of balance.

Interpreting TUG results

TUG time in seconds is the primary result. The interpretation depends on age and functional status.

For healthy adults under age 65, normal TUG time is typically under 10 seconds. Most people in this age group complete the test in 8-9 seconds with normal balance and mobility.

For healthy adults aged 65-79, normal TUG time is typically 10-12 seconds. Slowing begins in this age group, reflecting age-related changes in muscle function and balance.

For adults aged 80 and older, normal TUG time is typically 12-15 seconds. Greater slowing is expected at this age, though times consistently above 14 seconds warrant further assessment.

The critical cutoff for fall risk is 14 seconds. People who take more than 14 seconds to complete the TUG test have substantially elevated risk of falling within the next year. This cutoff has been validated in multiple populations and settings.

Results should be interpreted alongside other functional assessments. A TUG time of 15 seconds in a person with normal strength, normal gait speed, and good balance might be less concerning than a time of 13 seconds in someone with weakness and gait abnormality. Clinical judgment is important.

Limitations and when to combine with other tests

The TUG test is sensitive for mobility problems but not specific. A slow TUG time indicates impairment but does not identify the cause. Slowness could reflect weakness, poor balance, pain, or cognitive impairment.

Combining the TUG test with other assessments improves clinical utility. Grip strength testing identifies whether weakness is present. Gait speed assessment measures walking ability independently of other factors. Balance testing (such as the Romberg test or Functional Reach Test) identifies balance problems specifically.

The TUG test measures overall function but does not directly measure individual components. When a slow TUG result is identified, further focused assessment is needed to determine whether the problem is weakness, balance, gait, or cognitive.

The TUG test is less useful in people with significant cognitive impairment who may not understand instructions. It can be difficult or unsafe to perform in people with severe gait limitations or those requiring walking aids. For individuals with mobility aids, the TUG test can be performed using the aid, but results are less comparable to standard norms.

The test assumes a standard chair of normal height. Results may vary with different chair heights. Consistently using the same chair height improves test-retest reliability and allows meaningful tracking over time.

TUG within Able Assess

Able Assess incorporates the Timed Up and Go test within a comprehensive functional assessment battery. The TUG test is timed precisely using validated timing methods and interpreted against normative data.

Your Able Assess TUG result is compared to peers of your age and gender, showing whether your result falls in normal or at-risk range. The assessment also measures grip strength, gait speed, and other functional measures alongside TUG, providing a complete picture of your mobility and fall risk.

Serial Able Assess testing tracks TUG performance over time. Changes in TUG time of 2-3 seconds or more between tests indicate meaningful change in functional mobility. Able Assess testing enables clinicians to monitor whether function is stable, improving, or declining.

Combining TUG results with grip strength and gait speed measurement from Able Assess provides comprehensive assessment of functional mobility and fall risk. This integrated approach is more clinically informative than any single test alone.

stats.stat: TUG test cutoff of >14.2 seconds has 97% sensitivity for detecting mobility impairment
TUG >14 seconds is associated with 3-4 fold increased fall risk within 12 months
Healthy adults aged 65-79 typically complete TUG in 10-12 seconds
Combining TUG with grip strength and gait speed improves fall risk prediction over TUG alone
stats.source: European Union Geriatric Medicine Society position statement

items.question: Is a TUG time of 15 seconds normal?
items.answer: A TUG time of 15 seconds is at the threshold for elevated fall risk. For adults under age 75, this time suggests mild mobility impairment. For adults aged 80+, 15 seconds is at the upper end of normal. Context matters: a TUG of 15 seconds with normal strength might be less concerning than 13 seconds with significant weakness. Your doctor should interpret this alongside other findings.

items.question: Can I practice the TUG test to improve my time?
items.answer: Yes, regular physical activity improves TUG performance. Resistance training, particularly for legs, improves the strength component. Balance training improves coordination. Walking regularly improves gait speed. Improving these individual components through exercise translates to better TUG performance. This is beneficial for both functional ability and fall risk reduction.

items.question: What should I wear for the TUG test?
items.answer: Wear your usual everyday clothes and footwear. Good footwear with adequate support is important for safety and normal function. Do not wear slippers or unsupportive shoes, as these may artificially slow your time or increase fall risk. Wear any glasses or hearing aids you normally use.

items.question: Why is the TUG test better than asking if I'm falling?
items.answer: Many people who are at risk of falling do not realize it until they have a fall. The TUG test objectively measures mobility and identifies risk before a fall occurs. Additionally, people's perception of their own mobility is often inaccurate. Objective testing provides information that patient perception alone cannot.

items.question: How often should the TUG test be repeated?
items.answer: For routine fall risk assessment, TUG testing annually is appropriate for most adults over age 65. More frequent testing (every 6 months) may be warranted if you have known fall risk, recent falls, or conditions likely to affect mobility. Testing should be repeated whenever significant change in function is noticed.

blocks.heading: Assess your mobility with TUG testing
blocks.bodyText: Able Assess measures Timed Up and Go performance alongside grip strength and gait speed to identify fall risk. Early identification enables intervention before falls occur.
